long_thesis.english_markdown:
# Daily FI Market Intelligence - 2026-06-09

## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The report also separates price evidence from explanatory news. Treasury levels, oil, dollar, credit prices, and volatility define the close; central-bank, issuer, and sovereign stories explain why the next clearing test may be easier or harder. That separation prevents the thesis from treating every item as same-day causality.

The close also needs a sequencing lens: the first move came through checked market levels, the second through how policy and inflation compensation shaped those levels, and the third through whether credit and cross-assets accepted the move. That sequence makes the report easier to audit because each later section either broadens or narrows the opening read.

The opening balance also explains what is not being claimed. It does not say every headline moved the close; it says the close has to be read through the market channels that were active and the news that can change the next clearing level.
UST yields declined across the curve, led by the belly, while oil and breakevens softened enough to support the duration bid without erasing the month-to-date rise in front-end and real-yield compensation. The market state was not a clean all-clear because WTI 88.20, -3.40%, Brent 91.45, -2.97%, DXY 99.91, -0.14%, and VIX 19.87, +5.02% did different jobs across inflation, liquidity, and risk appetite. The opening read is therefore a conditional duration signal, with UST2Y 4.13%, -2 bp, UST10Y 4.53%, -3 bp, and 10Y breakeven 233 bp, -2 bp setting the price map while LQD 108.41, +0.32% and HYG 79.62, +0.10% show credit risk rather than cash-spread detail.

The opening frame stays balanced. A single close can show relief in duration without proving that policy risk, credit access, and dollar liquidity have all improved at once. The useful read is the ordering of channels: rates set the first impulse, inflation compensation explains part of the move, and credit plus cross-assets decide whether the signal is broad enough to matter beyond Treasuries.

That distinction keeps the note from forcing unrelated news into one causal chain. Some items affect the day directly through prices; others are background conditions for the next clearing test. The opening section separates those roles so the reader can see which facts changed the close and which facts only shape the next session.

The June 9 close therefore reads as a relief day with unfinished confirmation. The duration bid had support from softer energy and lower breakevens, but the move did not remove the policy debate around the front end or the financing debate around primary-market access. A cleaner bullish rates close would have come with calmer volatility, broader credit conviction, and less visible country-level pressure. Instead, the tape left a hierarchy: U.S. rates improved first, inflation compensation helped second, and credit, EM, and global duration still had to prove the move could survive beyond the Treasury curve.

The Fed channel stayed central because Kevin Warsh debate kept the front-end tied to whether policy at 3.5%-3.75% was restrictive enough. UST2Y 4.13%, -2 bp and UST5Y 4.26%, -3 bp left the front end and belly tied to the prior close, but the month-to-date gains in those yields kept policy restriction from looking settled. The curve at 2s10s 40 bp, -1 bp says the close was still debating soft-landing disinflation against a higher-for-longer front end.

Credit did not supply a simple risk-on verdict. LQD 108.41, +0.32%, HYG 79.62, +0.10%, JNK 95.92, +0.13%, and EMB 95.67, +0.41% are useful gauges, but the stronger information came from borrower and primary-market stories such as Tencent marketed dollar and offshore yuan bonds that could raise as much as $4.5 billion and Vedanta planned a $5.2 billion refinancing covering bonds and loans with 2028-2033 maturities. That combination points to functioning access for recognizable borrowers while weaker or more levered credits still need concessions.

Global duration widened the frame beyond Treasuries. BOJ watchers saw a June hike with possible follow-up by October and Hayakawa linked Japan's 2.8% 10-year yield high to inflation pressure kept the BOJ/ECB lane active, while Bank Indonesia's 25 bp move followed rupiah pressure and a 10-year yield surge toward 7.51% put EM rate defense on the same board as developed-market policy. Those items did not all cause the close, but they explain why the Treasury move cannot be read as a broad retreat from inflation risk.

Cross asset confirmation was mixed rather than decisive. WTI 88.20, -3.40% and Brent 91.45, -2.97% changed the inflation-compensation story, DXY 99.91, -0.14% tracked the dollar-liquidity channel, Gold 4,260, -1.75% captured the real-yield and haven read, and VIX 19.87, +5.02% showed the volatility backdrop. The close holds when those markets continue to validate the rates move.

The next market question is concrete: BOJ June decision and October guidance, Fed leadership debate around Warsh and front-end pricing, and Indonesia rupiah and 10-year yield stabilization must either support the close or expose it as a one-session move. Until those checks arrive, the fixed-income tape is market information about conditional easing or tightening in duration, not a completed turn in policy or credit.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

A useful curve read also avoids overclaiming precision. The front end reflects policy expectations, the belly reflects both policy and demand for intermediate duration, and the long end carries supply and fiscal compensation. The section keeps those channels distinct so a parallel move is not mistaken for one common driver.

The rates evidence also has to be read across horizons. A daily move can look small while the weekly or monthly change keeps policy risk alive. The report therefore keeps one-day direction, curve level, real-yield pressure, and breakeven behavior together instead of treating the nominal 10-year yield as the whole story.

The practical rates question is whether the next data point changes the policy path or only changes term premium. That distinction matters for curve trades, real-yield pressure, and how much room credit has to absorb all-in yield levels.
The UST board carried the day's main price signal: UST2Y 4.13%, -2 bp, UST5Y 4.26%, -3 bp, UST10Y 4.53%, -3 bp, and UST30Y 5.01%, -2 bp. The move was concentrated enough to matter for duration but not large enough to erase the recent shift in policy expectations. 2s10s 40 bp, -1 bp kept the curve discussion alive because front-end pricing and term premium did not move as one instrument.

For rates, the important distinction is composition. A move led by the front end says something different from a move led by the belly or long bond, and breakevens can either reinforce or dilute the nominal-yield message. The section therefore links policy pricing, real yields, and curve spreads before drawing any conclusion about duration demand.

Supply and liquidity remain part of the rates read only when the reporting gives a concrete handle. Auction demand, bill or coupon issuance, dealer balance-sheet capacity, and real-money sponsorship all belong here when present. When they are absent, the section keeps the emphasis on the checked price action rather than inventing a supply story.

Real-yield and inflation-compensation rows separated the mechanism. 10Y breakeven 233 bp, -2 bp and 10Y TIPS 2.20%, -1 bp show that the nominal move had an inflation-relief component, while the TIPS row kept real-rate restriction in the discussion. When breakevens move with nominal yields, the market is repricing the inflation premium embedded in the curve.

The Fed channel was still the anchor. Kevin Warsh debate kept the front-end tied to whether policy at 3.5%-3.75% was restrictive enough That made the front end sensitive to any data or leadership signal that could change the expected reaction function. A UST2Y 4.13%, -2 bp close does not prove policy is restrictive enough; it marks the immediate marginal pressure while keeping the monthly backup visible.

Supply and liquidity deserve a proportional read. Indonesia's off-cycle 25 bp BI-Rate increase to 5.5% showed how currency defense can spill into global duration Japan's budget package avoided additional calendar-year bond issuance even as Iran-related spending rose Those facts can influence long-end compensation, but a yield move alone is not auction absorption or dealer-balance-sheet evidence; supply claims stay tied to named auctions, issuance, fiscal plans, or liquidity events.

The curve quality was also important because the rally was not a full reset of the inflation or policy story. The front end and belly could rally on lower oil and calmer immediate inflation compensation, while the long end still had to price fiscal supply, overseas yield pressure, and investor appetite for duration near 5% on the 30-year. That is why the 2s10s row matters: it shows whether lower yields are easing financial conditions through the front end or simply repricing term premium around a still-restrictive policy regime.

The curve also carried cross-border pressure. BOJ watchers saw a June hike with possible follow-up by October and Hayakawa linked Japan's 2.8% 10-year yield high to inflation pressure kept JGB, ECB, and energy-inflation risk connected to UST term premium. The result is a rates section where the UST close leads, but global duration can still decide whether UST10Y 4.53%, -3 bp becomes a durable level or only an interim print.

The next rates check is Fed leadership debate around Warsh and front-end pricing. Confirmation would require front-end pricing, breakevens, and the belly to move in the same direction, while a challenge would be a renewed oil shock, weak auction demand, or a dollar-funding squeeze that steepens the curve for the wrong reason.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

The credit paragraph also distinguishes liquid marks from primary-market proof. Listed prices can react quickly to rates and equities, while new issues reveal how much yield, covenant protection, or maturity extension investors require. That is why borrower names and deal terms remain more informative than a broad risk label.

Primary absorption is the bridge between market prices and borrower reality. If stronger borrowers clear deals while weaker credits still require protections, the credit section reads as open but selective. That distinction is central for fixed income because all-in yield relief does not distribute evenly across the capital structure.

For credit, the important evidence is whether borrowers can clear risk without excessive structural give-up. If the visible deals need heavy concession, the rate move has not become broad financing relief.
Credit conditions were readable but not homogeneous. LQD 108.41, +0.32%, HYG 79.62, +0.10%, JNK 95.92, +0.13%, and EMB 95.67, +0.41% describe beta, yet the stronger signal came from primary-market and borrower-specific evidence. Tencent marketed dollar and offshore yuan bonds that could raise as much as $4.5 billion shows market access, while Vedanta planned a $5.2 billion refinancing covering bonds and loans with 2028-2033 maturities and Shutterfly sweetened a $1.15 billion junk-bond package with creditor protections show refinancing terms and creditor protections still matter.

Credit has to be read through access, not just index direction. Listed credit prices can show risk tone, but the stronger evidence comes from new-issue concessions, refinancing terms, maturity walls, and whether borrowers below the strongest tier can still raise money on workable terms.

The section also keeps borrower stories in their own lane. A large high-grade deal, a sponsor refinancing, a cross-border issuer funding print, and a stressed liability-management exercise all carry different information. Grouping them only as risk appetite would hide the funding detail that matters for fixed income.

Investment-grade access looked open where borrowers had recognizable scale, currency demand, or strategic sponsorship. Tencent marketed dollar and offshore yuan bonds that could raise as much as $4.5 billion and Vedanta planned a $5.2 billion refinancing covering bonds and loans with 2028-2033 maturities are not the same credit story, but both show investors still willing to evaluate duration and spread together. That is a funding-market state, not evidence that every spread segment tightened.

High-yield and direct-lending signals were more selective. Shutterfly sweetened a $1.15 billion junk-bond package with creditor protections. The point is borrower tiering: stronger names can still use windows, while weaker, governance-heavy, or refinancing-sensitive names need price, covenant, maturity, or structural concessions.

The duration bid or backup changes credit math through all-in yields. A lower Treasury base can help duration-heavy IG bonds, but HY access still depends on spread compensation, refinancing runway, and lender confidence. That is why HYG 79.62, +0.10% and JNK 95.92, +0.13% remain gauges, not standalone evidence of cash-spread relief.

Primary-market absorption also interacts with supply. If official funding calendars, corporate M&A financing, and AI-infrastructure borrowing all compete for duration, credit can look open while demanding more precise concessions. The relevant check is not only whether LQD 108.41, +0.32% is higher, but whether new deals clear without widening concessions.

The issuer mix kept the credit conclusion deliberately narrow. Tencent's planned dollar and offshore-yuan supply pointed to demand for recognizable Asian credit, while Vedanta's refinancing package and Shutterfly's creditor-protection changes showed that investors were still sorting borrowers by governance, leverage, and refinancing urgency. That is not a closed market, but it is also not broad easing. The fixed-income relevance is the price of access: lower Treasury yields can help coupon math, yet weaker structures still have to pay in protection, maturity design, or concession.

Forward credit risk sits in the names and structures: Tencent and Vedanta pricing after bookbuilding and oil and breakevens after the Iran risk premium faded. A constructive read needs market access to survive after the rates move, not just ETF price gains on a single close.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Global duration is especially important when inflation and fiscal news point in different directions. A central bank may sound cautious even as growth slows, and a finance ministry may increase supply even as investors want duration. The section keeps those offsets visible because they affect term premium.

The developed-market comparison matters most when policy messages diverge. A U.S. duration bid can be diluted by overseas central-bank caution, fiscal supply, or hedging costs. The section therefore treats foreign sovereign markets as part of the clearing-price discussion rather than background color.

For global duration, local reaction functions matter because the same inflation shock can be priced differently across regions. That is why the section keeps policy language, fiscal supply, and investor demand together.
Global duration had its own policy story. BOJ watchers saw a June hike with possible follow-up by October Hayakawa linked Japan's 2.8% 10-year yield high to inflation pressure That leaves BOJ, ECB, gilt, bund, and JGB markets as active inputs to the UST term-premium discussion rather than passive reflections of the U.S. close.

Global duration is a constraint on the U.S. read, not a decorative add-on. JGBs, gilts, bunds, and other core markets can either validate a U.S. duration move or reveal that the U.S. close was more local, technical, or policy-specific than the headline yield change suggests.

Central-bank and fiscal news belong here when they change the clearing price for sovereign duration. The section separates domestic inflation reaction functions, budget supply, and currency-hedged demand, because each can push long-end yields even when near-term growth news points the other way.

The BOJ lane mattered because Japan's inflation and fiscal mix can affect global duration supply and currency hedging. A JGB market facing higher policy rates, fiscal sensitivity, or weaker auction demand forces investors to compare UST compensation against yen hedging and local rate risk.

The ECB lane mattered because energy inflation can push a central bank toward tightening even when growth forecasts weaken. ECB officials discussed an energy-driven inflation shock and a possible first hike since 2023 That combination can flatten or cheapen European duration in a way that eventually feeds dollar curves.

Fiscal transmission stayed visible. Japan's extra budget included Iran-conflict reserves without new calendar-year bond issuance Sovereign issuance does not automatically cause a UST selloff, but it changes the clearing price investors require for long-duration assets when risk budgets are finite.

The developed-market read is therefore not simply synchronized easing or synchronized tightening. It is a set of local policy reaction functions being forced to price energy, fiscal spending, currency pressure, and growth at different speeds.

The next DM duration check is BOJ June decision and October guidance. If BOJ or ECB guidance hardens while UST breakevens are already firming, the global duration complex can challenge the U.S. rally; if oil keeps falling and policy language softens, the UST bid has more room.

The Japan lane matters most because it links inflation, fiscal choices, and hedged demand. A higher JGB yield can make U.S. duration less compelling for yen-based investors after hedging, even when the U.S. close itself looks constructive. The fiscal note also matters because a budget plan that avoids near-term new issuance can calm one supply channel without changing the larger inflation and policy debate. For USTs, that means overseas duration can validate a rally only if local central-bank guidance and investor demand stop pushing in the opposite direction.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

The sovereign section also treats dollar liquidity as a transmission channel rather than a background variable. A stable dollar can let hard-currency spreads absorb lower U.S. yields; a firmer dollar can force local policy defense and keep external borrowers exposed even when Treasuries rally.

Sovereign funding also requires a calendar view. A country can benefit from lower U.S. yields today and still face refinancing pressure if reserves, commodity income, or multilateral support weaken. The section keeps those channels visible so EM is not reduced to a single external-debt price.

For EM, country dispersion is not noise. It is the mechanism through which U.S. rates, the dollar, commodity income, and debt-management credibility become different outcomes for different borrowers.
EM fixed income carried a separate risk map. Bank Indonesia's 25 bp move followed rupiah pressure and a 10-year yield surge toward 7.51% Tencent demand showed Asian high-grade access remained open The section starts from EM rates, FX, and hard-currency credit because those markets can diverge from UST duration when local policy credibility or dollar access dominates.

EM transmission depends on the dollar and country balance sheets. Lower U.S. yields can help hard-currency bonds, but local curves still need currency stability, credible policy, and manageable external funding needs before the relief travels into domestic markets.

The section therefore treats EMB-style price action as only the first layer. Debt-management decisions, reserve use, multilateral support, commodity exposure, and local policy defense explain why one sovereign can rally while another still pays for dollar liquidity stress.

Dollar liquidity was visible through both the DXY row and country-specific funding stories. DXY 99.91, -0.14% gives the broad market handle; individual borrowers and sovereigns then decide whether that dollar backdrop feels manageable or punitive.

Indonesia remained the clearest currency-defense case. Rate hikes, rupiah moves, bond yields, fund outflows, and official budget messaging all pointed to a market trying to decide how much yield is enough to compensate for policy uncertainty.

Hard-currency credit had more than one face. Taiwan five-year yields reached the highest since 2008 as local liquidity tightened Zambia received enough support for a 2053 dollar-bond buyback after improving terms Those items belong in EM because they speak to sovereign credibility, dollar access, and investor willingness to absorb country or issuer risk outside the U.S.

The EM lesson is that lower U.S. yields can open a window without solving domestic pressure. Indonesia still had to defend the currency and persuade investors that local yields compensated for policy uncertainty. Zambia's debt-management path pointed to a different channel, where improved buyback terms and creditor support matter more than a one-day UST rally. Taiwan's local-yield pressure showed a third channel, where domestic liquidity and issuance conditions can tighten even when dollar conditions are not the main stress. Those differences are the point of the EM section.

The EMB gauge at EMB 95.67, +0.41% is useful, but it cannot summarize country dispersion. A stronger EMB close can coexist with stress in Indonesia, Senegal, Zambia, Brazil, Turkey, or other sovereign lanes when the underlying driver is local policy credibility.

The next EM check is Indonesia rupiah and 10-year yield stabilization. Confirmation would be tighter sovereign risk, calmer FX, and open dollar books; challenge would be renewed rupiah weakness, higher local yields, or funding terms that require visible concessions.

## Cross asset

Cross-asset evidence also guards against false comfort. Softer oil can lower inflation compensation, but risk appetite still depends on equities, volatility, credit pricing, and the dollar. A clean fixed-income read needs those markets to move in compatible directions, not simply one supportive commodity print.

The cross-asset read is strongest when inflation, risk appetite, and liquidity move together. Oil can help breakevens, but equities, volatility, the dollar, and gold decide whether that help becomes broader financial-condition relief. Contradictions are preserved because they are often the next session’s signal.

For cross-assets, the best signal is breadth rather than any single price. The rates read is stronger when oil, volatility, equities, credit, gold, and the dollar do not pull in opposite directions.
Cross asset evidence gave the FI read both support and tension. WTI 88.20, -3.40% and Brent 91.45, -2.97% shaped the inflation impulse, DXY 99.91, -0.14% framed dollar liquidity, Gold 4,260, -1.75% gave the real-yield and haven signal, and VIX 19.87, +5.02% showed volatility risk.

Cross-assets decide whether the rates move is benign or defensive. Oil and breakevens speak to inflation compensation; equities and volatility speak to risk appetite; the dollar and gold speak to real-rate pressure and global liquidity. The section keeps those signals separate before judging breadth.

This is also where contradictions stay visible. A Treasury rally alongside soft oil and firm equities is different from a Treasury rally alongside weaker equities and higher volatility. The section preserves that tension instead of turning every market into confirmation of the same thesis.

Oil was the cleanest inflation channel. When WTI and Brent fall, breakevens can compress and duration can rally; when they rebound, the same market can rebuild inflation compensation even if growth sentiment is steady.

The dollar channel was quieter but still important. DXY 99.91, -0.14% says broad dollar pressure was not the dominant close-level shock, yet EM funding and FX-defense stories show why even a small DXY move can matter for sovereign spreads and local rates.

Equities and volatility did not deliver a single message. S&P 500 7,386.65, -0.26% and Nasdaq 100 29,084.50, -1.12% kept risk appetite in view, while VIX 19.87, +5.02% showed whether investors were demanding protection. The FI read is stronger when equity resilience, lower oil, and calmer volatility all point in the same direction.

Gold added a real-yield and haven check. Gold 4,260, -1.75% must be read beside TIPS and breakevens, not in isolation; a gold rally with firmer real yields says something different from a gold decline alongside lower inflation compensation.

The cross-asset mix therefore kept the close from becoming a simple risk-on story. Softer crude helped inflation compensation and supported duration, but weaker equities and higher volatility meant the market was also paying for uncertainty. The dollar was not a major shock on the day, yet it remained relevant because EM funding and hard-currency sovereign risk can deteriorate even when the headline dollar index is quiet. The more durable version of the rates move would have lower oil, stable equities, calmer volatility, and no renewed dollar squeeze at the same time.

The cross-asset test into the next session is whether oil, DXY, VIX, equities, gold, and breakevens keep validating the rates move. If one channel breaks, the fixed-income close becomes more fragile.

## Forward Market Significance

The forward section therefore gives the next report a disciplined handoff. It identifies what would broaden the thesis, what would narrow it, and what would leave the close mostly unchanged. That makes the daily sequence cumulative without forcing certainty into days where the evidence is mixed.

The forward checks are intentionally observable: data prints, auction demand, issuer concessions, dollar moves, and cross-asset breadth can all be checked against the next close. That makes the report iterative rather than declarative, with each future tape either extending, narrowing, or reversing the current read.

For the next tape, the goal is to update the hierarchy rather than defend today’s conclusion. The relevant question is which channel moves first and whether the others join, lag, or contradict it.
BOJ June decision and October guidance is the opening market trigger on the board. Confirmation would come from pricing, curve shape, FX, and credit behaving consistently after the event; a challenge would be a reversal in UST10Y 4.53%, -3 bp, 10Y breakeven 233 bp, -2 bp, or WTI 88.20, -3.40%.

The forward section works as a conclusion; it is a checklist. It names the data, supply, issuer, policy, dollar, and cross-asset channels that can change the next close. That keeps the report actionable as market information without turning it into a trading recommendation.

The most useful forward read is conditional. If inflation, auctions, funding terms, and cross-assets line up, the close can broaden. If they split, the next report narrow the thesis rather than force coherence. The section gives that next update a clean audit trail.

The June 9 handoff is therefore less about defending a directional view and more about tracking which channel confirms first. If BOJ guidance, Fed-leadership debate, Indonesia stabilization, and primary-market pricing all calm together, the Treasury rally can broaden into a cleaner fixed-income relief signal. If oil rebounds, the dollar firms, or issuer concessions widen, the same close becomes a one-day rates repricing with limited credit transmission. That distinction is the key forward value of the report: it preserves the difference between a price move, a macro explanation, and a validated financing environment.

Fed leadership debate around Warsh and front-end pricing matters because policy pricing remains the bridge between inflation data and the front end. A hawkish read would pressure UST2Y 4.13%, -2 bp and UST5Y 4.26%, -3 bp, while a softer read would need breakevens and real yields to confirm rather than only equities.

Indonesia rupiah and 10-year yield stabilization is the country or funding stress check. EM confirmation needs calmer FX and sovereign yields; renewed pressure would shift attention from the UST close to dollar funding and country-risk premia.

Tencent and Vedanta pricing after bookbuilding is the issuer and primary-market test. A healthy market clears new deals with stable concessions and transparent funding plans; a weaker market forces higher coupons, tighter covenants, delayed offerings, or maturity-extension negotiations.

oil and breakevens after the Iran risk premium faded decides whether cross-asset inflation relief is durable. Oil, DXY, gold, equities, VIX, and breakevens need to point in the same direction for the fixed-income read to remain coherent.

Dates to watch: 2026-06-10, 2026-06-16, 2026-06-17, 2026-06-24. The important outcome is not a single level by itself, but whether the curve, inflation compensation, credit access, dollar liquidity, and global duration all confirm the same market state.

section_summaries.summary: The opening read ties the day's rates move to inflation compensation, dollar conditions, and credit absorption. It keeps the close focused on what traded, while leaving unrelated headlines as context rather than forced causality.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Frames the session's dominant fixed-income move.

section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: The rates section separates front-end policy pricing from intermediate and long-end duration demand. It uses Treasury levels, curve spreads, real yields, and breakevens to explain whether the close was policy repricing or term-premium pressure.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Shows whether policy pricing or term premium led.

section_summaries.summary: The credit section reads funding through borrower access, issuance terms, and listed credit prices. It avoids treating index moves as proof by themselves, so primary-market concessions and refinancing quality remain the key absorption checks.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Tests whether rates pressure reached borrower funding.

section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: The global duration section compares the U.S. move with non-U.S. policy signals, sovereign supply, and fiscal pressure. It shows whether overseas curves reinforced the close or kept the rates move locally constrained.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Checks whether non-U.S. duration joined the U.S. move.

section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: The EM section follows dollar liquidity, local policy defense, and hard-currency funding access. It distinguishes sovereign-specific stories from broad EM transmission, especially when DXY or external financing costs tighten conditions.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Tracks sovereign funding and dollar-liquidity pressure.

section_summaries.summary: The cross-asset section compares oil, equities, volatility, gold, the dollar, and credit prices against the Treasury move. It explains whether the rates signal eased financial conditions or remained narrow and selective.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Separates inflation relief from broader risk tone.

section_summaries.summary: The forward section turns the close into observable checks across inflation data, Treasury supply, credit concessions, global policy, EM funding, and cross-assets. It states what would broaden or narrow the next fixed-income read.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Defines the data and supply checks for the next tape.

methodology_note: Synthesized market analysis from market levels and reporting context. Forward Market Significance carries the next market checks.
